items.evidence_text:
Client: Thanks. I’m Daniel. Post-divorce I just want to make sure I’m not missing anything—like taxes, retirement, and that my accounts aren’t a mess.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your gross income in a typical year?
Client: Gross is 214900 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And that’s mainly from your self-employed work, right?
Client: Yeah, self-employed. The income can be lumpy month to month, but that annual number is right.

Client: Yeah, self-employed. The income can be lumpy month to month, but that annual number is right.
Advisor: Okay. On the spending side, what do your total monthly expenses usually run—housing, living costs, everything?
Client: Around nine grand most months. The exact total monthly expenses number I have is 9050.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. Now debt: any monthly payments we need to plan around—mortgage, car loan, credit cards, anything like that?
Client: No. Monthly debt payments total is 0.

Client: Thanks. I’m Daniel. Post-divorce I just want to make sure I’m not missing anything—like taxes, retirement, and that my accounts aren’t a mess.
Advisor: Totally fair. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow—what’s your gross income in a typical year?
Client: Gross is 214900 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And that’s mainly from your self-employed work, right?
Client: Yeah, self-employed. The income can be lumpy month to month, but that annual number is right.

Client: Yes, no mortgage. The house is paid off.
Advisor: Great. Let’s shift to what you’ve built up. Roughly how much do you have in investable accounts altogether—excluding the house value?
Client: Investable assets total is 649650.
Advisor: One sec—let me note that down. And those investable accounts are spread across a few buckets, right? Like a taxable account, retirement accounts, and some cash at the bank?
Client: Yeah, that’s right.

Client: And there’s a private markets investment—52950.
Advisor: Thanks. And then separate from investable assets, your primary residence—what’s your best estimate on the home value?
Client: 314500.
Advisor: Great. That helps me see the full balance sheet.
Advisor: Just to close the loop on liabilities—besides no mortgage, no other loans outstanding at all?

Advisor: Okay. Any dependents we should plan around—kids or anyone financially dependent on you?
Client: No—0 dependents.
Advisor: Perfect. When we talk about investing and the plan overall, how would you describe your risk tolerance—conservative, moderate, aggressive, somewhere in there?
Client: Moderate.
Advisor: Helpful. And when markets drop, what’s the part that tends to bug you most—seeing the account value swing, or worrying you’ll have to change your lifestyle?

Advisor: Hi Daniel—good to meet you. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what would make this meeting a win for you?

Client: Yes, NY.

Client: Divorced.
Advisor: Okay. Any dependents we should plan around—kids or anyone financially dependent on you?

Advisor: Perfect. Now taxes—when you think about your marginal bracket, where do you usually land? Like the 22, 24, 32 range—what’s the right band for you?
Client: 24%.
Advisor: Okay. And just so I’m not assuming—are you filing in the U.S. system only, no cross-border stuff?
Client: Correct, just US.

Advisor: Okay. And just so I’m not assuming—are you filing in the U.S. system only, no cross-border stuff?
Client: Correct, just US.
Advisor: Great. Switching gears slightly—how are you looking to work with an advisor, Daniel? Some folks want a one-time plan, others want ongoing management through an RIA—registered investment advisor. What are you aiming for?
Client: I want ongoing management through an RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Okay, that helps. And you’re good with us coordinating the implementation—moving pieces as needed—but keeping it simple and transparent?

Advisor: Okay. And on the income number itself—what are we using as your gross annual income these days?

Client: And there’s a private markets investment—52950.

Advisor: Helpful. And when markets drop, what’s the part that tends to bug you most—seeing the account value swing, or worrying you’ll have to change your lifestyle?
Client: Mostly the swings. I can stay invested, but I don’t want surprises—like, I want the guardrails we talked about.
Advisor: Makes sense. One more quick thing while I’m noting this—your income is self-employed, and you mentioned interest and dividends coming in monthly. Is that still the main setup?
Client: Yeah, that’s right—self-employed, and I do have interest and dividends coming in monthly.
Advisor: Great. And on the assets we covered: you’ve got that taxable account at 402850, the old 401(k)/IRA bucket at 135400, bank savings 58500, private markets 52950, and the home value 314500—no loans and no insurance policies right now. Did I miss anything big?

Advisor: Thanks. When you say gross, that’s before taxes and before any business expenses you might deduct, right?
Client: Yeah—before taxes. Expenses are separate.
Advisor: Perfect. One admin thing: in our notes, we label people as Client 1, Client 2, etc. Since it’s just you, you’re Client 1—does that match what you expect?
Client: Yes—1.
Advisor: Great. And just to connect it back to what you said earlier—self-employed income, plus you mentioned interest and dividends coming in monthly—still true, nothing new to add there?

Advisor: Okay. So next time we can talk through whether you need coverage and what amount makes sense, but we’ll keep it aligned with your goals.

Advisor: Before we move on, anything about the accounts—like where they’re held—that you think is important? We don’t need account numbers, just the general providers.
Client: Nothing special. They’re spread around—some on a retirement platform, some with an insurance company, and one at a brokerage.
Advisor: Perfect. We’ll map that out and then talk strategy—taxes, investment mix, and a plan you can stick with when markets get choppy.
Client: That’s exactly what I want—guardrails and a clear plan.
